responses: I live in Johnson County Wyoming with my wife and two children. Like over one-half of the current Wyoming residents I was born in another state, in my case Virginia, but I moved here first as a young man and again permanently over five years ago.

I am a retired big game and small game biologist who spent three decades managing game populations. For the last six years, I have worked as a National Director and Expert for Gun Owners of America protecting American's Right to Keep and Bear Arms. I have B.S. and M.S. degrees and over 30 years of experience in governmental policy. I am a strong believer in the United States and Wyoming Constitutions and in the right of all people to be left alone by an out-of-control government.

Some of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming include: young people leaving Wyoming for other states, maintaining enough water for current and future needs, federal overreach into every aspect of our lives, and threats against our core economic engines of coal, oil, gas, other mining, agriculture, etc. As a State Legislator I would work to reduce taxes to keep Wyoming affordable and fight regulations that stifle free enterprise and all businesses.

We need to remove unnecessary regulations that make healthcare more expensive. Many people forget that Obamacare changed American healthcare in catastrophic ways, and the state needs to work to get back to a system that allowed doctors and companies to operate without the excessive government rules and costs. I had a doctor friend who retired rather than spend millions to comply with the excessive regulations forced upon doctors by Obamacare.

I do not support efforts to sell public lands. I do believe federal lands need to be managed better - especially in terms of forest management which has been neglected for decades. I would work for state actions that could facilitate better cooperation between the state and federal government to ensure Wyoming residents benefited from public lands for hunting, fishing, hiking, camping, and other recreation. I would also work towards land swaps when those were beneficial for both the private landowner and the public. These would not be forced upon anyone, but there are cases where both the private landowner and the pubic could benefit from trading lands.
I would work to cut waste, fraud, and abuse to ensure our state workers can be compensated fairly and that we can fund essential services like roads, bridges, police, fire, and rescue.

The government should not pick winners and losers in business. It is wrong to use taxpayers money to support industry X while ignoring industry Y. We need the Wyoming legislature to remove unnecessary taxes and regulations on all businesses to allow those businesses to thrive on their own. For example, I have a close friend with a large company in Buffalo, and he would hire several high-paying positions immediately if not for unnecessary state restrictions on his business.

I ran in 2024 and raised 98% of my money from Wyoming donors. My opponent, the current incumbent, took tens of thousands of dollars in Political Action Committee (PAC) money. My 2024 records prove that I stand with the citizens rather than with the power-brokers.
I live in Buffalo, Wyoming. I have lived in Buffalo for over 30 years. I was born in Worland, Wyoming.
I will be 71.

I am a lifelong Wyomingite, raised in Johnson and Sheridan counties, where I learned the values of hard work, personal responsibility, and service to others. I raised my children in Buffalo. I understand how important it is to keep Wyoming a place where future generations can live, work, and raise their families. For three decades, I have served Wyoming through public service and community leadership, including a Johnson County Commissioner, a CNA, AEMT, Public Health Response Coordinator, Emergency Management Coordinator, and Public Information Officer for local fire districts. These experiences have given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing Wyoming families, farmers and ranchers, healthcare providers, emergency responders, and local governments. I am running to bring practical experience, fiscal responsibility, and conservative Wyoming point of view to the Legislature while protecting our freedoms, strengthening our communities, and securing Wyoming’s future.

Wyoming's greatest challenge is ensuring long-term economic stability.   As markets change and federal policies continue to impact industries, we must protect our core economic drivers while encouraging responsible diversification and innovation.

Another challenge is retaining and attracting young families, skilled workers, healthcare professionals, and first responders.  We must create opportunities that allow them to stay and succeed here.

Protecting Wyoming's water is another challenge. I would support our agencies with additional resources to meet this challenge.

Wyoming can capitalize on its strong work ethic, independent attitude, abundant natural resources, low-tax environment.  I will support policies that strengthen our energy and agricultural sectors, promote responsible economic growth, improve workforce development, and ensure state government lives within its means.

I will focus on practical solutions, local control, and responsible stewardship of taxpayer dollars.

Wyomingites struggle with rising insurance costs, long travel distances for care, and shortages of healthcare professionals.

 We must support rural hospitals, clinics, EMS services, and long-term care facilities that are often the backbone of healthcare in our communities. Also, we should continue reducing barriers to recruiting and retaining healthcare professionals, including nurses, physicians, mental health providers, and emergency responders.

We should review regulations and mandates that unnecessarily increase healthcare costs.   It is also important to support the efforts of the Wyoming Department of Health to manage and distribute the Rural Health Transformation funds.

My work experience has given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing patients and providers. As a legislator, I will work to strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce, support rural healthcare infrastructure, promote innovation.

Wyoming's public lands support ranching, energy production, recreation, wildlife habitat, tourism, and the outdoor traditions that define our way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell public lands that would reduce public access or limit opportunities for future generations.

As a legislator, I will support multiple-use management that balances responsible energy and mineral development, healthy forests and rangelands, wildlife conservation, and public access. I also support stronger coordination among federal agencies, state and local governments, and private landowners to ensure decisions reflect local knowledge and Wyoming values.

The goal is balanced stewardship. By emphasizing local input, responsible management, and long-term planning, we can ensure Wyoming's public lands continue to benefit the people who live here today and future generations.

Property tax relief is warranted, but it must be balanced with the reality that counties, cities, schools, and special districts rely on those revenues to provide essential services.
 As a legislator, I will support a thoughtful approach that provides meaningful tax relief while carefully evaluating the impact on local governments and taxpayers. 
I would advocate for a state backfill or stabilization framework to mitigate revenue losses in counties with limited alternatives, particularly those without strong mineral production or tourism-based economies.
Any property tax relief should be accompanied by transparency and accountability regarding how lost revenue will affect services such as public safety, roads, senior services, and local infrastructure. We must avoid simply shifting costs from one group of taxpayers to another or creating long-term budget shortfalls.
Wyoming taxpayers deserve both tax relief and responsible government that lives within its means.

The Legislature should not attempt to direct the economy but instead create the conditions for private-sector growth, support local decision-making, and ensure Wyoming remains a place where businesses can invest, expand, and create good-paying jobs.
The Legislature should focus on maintaining Wyoming’s competitive advantages—low taxes, reasonable regulation, reliable infrastructure investment, and a strong commitment to property rights. These factors are essential for attracting and retaining businesses and families.
The Legislature can help by investing in workforce development, career and technical education, and partnerships between schools, community colleges, and industry. A skilled workforce is one of the most important factors in long-term economic growth.

Wyoming’s campaign finance system should be regularly reviewed to ensure transparency, public trust, and fairness in elections. Overall, I believe voters have a right to know who is funding political campaigns and how money is being spent.
If improvements are needed, they should focus on modernization, efficiency, and clarity so that Wyoming voters can easily see who is influencing elections and make informed decisions at the ballot box.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
